sourceTitle: Make work more efficient with database button properties

questions.question: Which of the following is the first step to add a database button property to a Notion database?

options.text: Open the / menu and select Button
options.explanation: The / menu creates a button block, not a database property.

options.text: Select + New property in the database’s properties menu
options.explanation: Creating a new property is the initial step for adding a button property.

options.text: Click Add a step on an existing button
options.explanation: You need a button property first before you can add steps.

options.text: Choose Database automation from the automation menu
options.explanation: Database automations are a different type of automation, not a property.

questions.question: What does the "Show confirmation" step do when configured on a database button?

options.text: It automatically sends a Slack notification after the button is clicked
options.explanation: Slack notifications are only possible with database automations, not confirmation dialogs.

options.text: It displays a dialog asking the user to confirm or cancel the action
options.explanation: The confirmation step shows a dialog that acts as a safeguard before proceeding.

options.text: It creates a new page in the selected database
options.explanation: Creating a new page is done with the "Add page to…" step.

options.text: It opens the page that was edited by the button
options.explanation: Opening a page uses the "Open page" step, not the confirmation step.

questions.question: Which action step would you use to automatically assign the person who clicked a button to a property called "Approved by"?

options.text: Edit pages in… → Edit a property → set "Approved by" to "Person who clicked the button"
options.explanation: You edit the current page and set the property to the person who clicked the button.

options.text: Add page to… → select a template that includes the "Approved by" field
options.explanation: Adding a new page doesn’t automatically capture the clicking user.

options.text: Open page → manually select the approver
options.explanation: Opening a page does not assign a property.

options.text: Show confirmation → choose "Approved by" from the dialog
options.explanation: Confirmation dialogs only confirm the action; they don’t set properties.

questions.question: When would you prefer using a Database automation over a Database button?

options.text: When you want a workflow to run automatically after a property changes, without any user clicks
options.explanation: Database automations trigger on property changes and run without manual clicks.

options.text: When you need a one‑click button that updates several properties at once
options.explanation: That’s exactly what a Database button is designed for.

options.text: When you want to generate a checklist on a page
options.explanation: Generating checklists is typical for Button blocks, not automations.

options.text: When you need to open a specific page after clicking
options.explanation: Opening a page can be done with a Database button; automations don’t open pages for the user.

questions.question: Which combination of steps would you need to create an "Escalate" button that tags a manager, sets priority to high, and asks for confirmation?

options.text: Edit pages in… (tag manager), Edit a property (set priority), Show confirmation
options.explanation: All three steps together achieve the desired escalation workflow.

options.text: Add page to… (create new task), Open page, Show confirmation
options.explanation: Adding a new page isn’t needed to tag the manager or set priority on the existing task.

options.text: Show confirmation, Open page, Edit pages in… (set priority)
options.explanation: The manager tag is missing; also order matters but not the main issue.

options.text: Edit pages in… (set priority), Add page to… (new page), Open page
options.explanation: This lacks the manager tag and the confirmation step.

questions.question: What is a key limitation of a Button block compared to a Database button?

options.text: Button blocks cannot create new pages in any database
options.explanation: Button blocks can create new pages in databases.

options.text: Button blocks live on a page and cannot be added as a property to each row of a database
options.explanation: Button blocks are page‑level elements, not row‑level properties like Database buttons.

options.text: Button blocks cannot edit any properties of a database page
options.explanation: Button blocks can edit properties, though they’re less suited for bulk row actions.

options.text: Button blocks require a Slack integration to work
options.explanation: Slack integration is only for Database automations, not Button blocks.

questions.question: If you want a button to create a new task in a separate ‘Tasks’ database and immediately set its due date to today, which sequence of steps should you configure?

options.text: Add page to… (Tasks database) → Edit pages in… (New page) → Edit a property (Due date = Now)
options.explanation: First you add the page, then edit that newly created page to set the due date.

options.text: Edit pages in… (Current page) → Add page to… (Tasks database) → Show confirmation
options.explanation: Editing the current page won’t affect the new task’s due date.

options.text: Show confirmation → Add page to… (Tasks database) → Open page
options.explanation: Confirmation doesn’t set any property values.

options.text: Open page → Edit a property (Due date = Now) → Add page to… (Tasks database)
options.explanation: You need to edit the newly added page, not the page you opened.

questions.question: Why might you choose a Database button instead of a Database automation for a workflow that requires user consent before proceeding?

options.text: Because Database buttons can display a confirmation dialog that forces the user to approve the action
options.explanation: Confirmation dialogs are only available on buttons, ensuring user consent.

options.text: Because Database automations can pause execution until a user clicks a button
options.explanation: Automations run automatically; they cannot wait for user interaction.

options.text: Because Database buttons are faster to execute than automations
options.explanation: Speed isn’t the reason; the key factor is the need for explicit user confirmation.

options.text: Because Database automations cannot edit multiple properties at once
options.explanation: Automations can edit multiple properties; the limitation is the lack of user confirmation.

questions.question: In an upvote system using a Database button, which property configuration ensures the total number of upvotes is calculated correctly?

options.text: Edit the current page to add the clicking user to a multi‑person "Upvoted by" property; a formula column then counts entries
options.explanation: Adding the user to a multi‑person property and using a formula to count yields the upvote total.

options.text: Create a new page for each upvote and sum the pages with a rollup
options.explanation: Creating new pages is unnecessary and complicates counting.

options.text: Use a confirmation dialog to increment a numeric "Upvote count" property
options.explanation: Confirmation dialogs don’t modify numeric properties automatically.

options.text: Set the button to open the page and manually increase a number field
options.explanation: Manual increments defeat the purpose of automation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the relationship between Database buttons, Button blocks, and Database automations?

options.text: All three can send Slack notifications, but only Database automations can edit properties without user interaction
options.explanation: Only Database automations can send Slack messages; buttons cannot.

options.text: Database buttons and Button blocks both require a user click to run, while Database automations run automatically based on property changes
options.explanation: Buttons need clicks; automations trigger on changes without clicks.

options.text: Button blocks are a type of Database button that lives inside a database view
options.explanation: Button blocks live on pages, not inside database rows.

options.text: Database automations can only add new pages, whereas Database buttons can only edit existing pages
options.explanation: Both can add and edit pages; the main difference is trigger mechanism.
